areas.name: Economic Growth & Job Creation
areas.summary: Oregon's Prosperity Roadmap / Governor's Prosperity Council — fast-track permits for major projects, prepare industrial sites, modernize business incentives.

chapters.reasoning: Business Oregon — state loans/grants/incentive programs; the most direct match to "modernize business incentives."

DEQ issues the environmental permits (air/water/land) that "fast-track permits for major projects" would need to streamline. Broad chapter — not every DEQ rule is permit-timeline-related, but permitting is a large share of it.

areas.name: Housing & Homelessness
areas.summary: Multi-pronged approach to housing production — funding for housing infrastructure, affordable rental construction, home repair for older adults.

chapters.reasoning: OHCS is the state housing-production/homelessness-funding agency — the direct implementer of this priority.
Building code rules directly gate how fast housing (and repairs) can be built/permitted.

DLCD sets regional housing-needs and housing-production-strategy rules (also touches industrial land use, but housing production is the more specific, currently active mandate — kept out of economic_growth to avoid double-counting the same chapter in two areas).

areas.name: Mental Health & Addiction
areas.summary: Remove barriers for qualified behavioral health/addiction professionals — streamline credentialing, reduce administrative burdens.

chapters.reasoning: OHA's behavioral-health program rules — direct service-delivery implementer.
OHA's addiction-services program rules — direct service-delivery implementer, explicitly named in the priority ("addiction").
Licenses counselors/therapists — the credentialing-streamlining target named in the priority.
Licenses social workers, a substantial share of the behavioral-health workforce — same credentialing-streamlining rationale.

areas.name: Defending Oregon Communities
areas.summary: Protecting vulnerable residents, core services, and civil liberties from federal funding cuts and aggressive immigration enforcement.
areas.caveat: The weakest fit of the four to routine OAR rulemaking — this priority is substantially about litigation posture, funding decisions, and administrative guidance (much of it never becomes a numbered rule), not chapter-level rule amendments. Included for completeness; read its rulemaking-activity signal with that caveat, more than the other three areas.

chapters.reasoning: DOJ is the state's legal-defense function — the most direct link to "defending" against federal action, though DOJ's chapter covers routine practice rules (e.g. public records, victims' rights) more than litigation posture, which isn't rulemaking at all.
DHS administers core services (SNAP, TANF, child welfare) for the "vulnerable residents" this priority names as needing protection.
BOLI enforces employment civil-rights protections — the closest rulemaking-adjacent link to "civil liberties".

note: CURATED, NOT mechanically derived. Maps OAR chapters to 4 stated gubernatorial priority areas (as given by the repo maintainer, 2026-07-22), so build_governor_priorities.py can show rulemaking recency/volume in each area. This is an interpretive judgment call about which agencies/chapters plausibly implement each priority — not something the corpus itself asserts. A chapter appears in at most one area (even where a real dual-purpose exists, e.g. DLCD's land-use rules touch both industrial siting and housing production) to keep each area's story distinct rather than double-counted. Review before treating as authoritative; update citations/reasoning if the mapping should change.
viz_note: Rulemaking recency in each area's CURATED chapter mapping (see _meta/catalog/governor-priorities.yml), vs. the corpus-wide OAR baseline. A recent-activity spike is consistent with active attention, not proof of it — and the absence of one doesn't mean inaction (funding/staffing/litigation moves don't show up as rulemaking at all). Non-authoritative.
